summary: Fowler reflects on how IntelliJ IDEA fundamentally changed his expectations for development tools when he first encountered it in late 2000. While initially drawn to its refactoring support, he was more impressed by its use of an in-memory parse tree to provide intelligent code completion and suggestions. The tool was so compelling that it unified Thoughtworks developers who had previously fought over IDE preferences. Fowler considers IntelliJ the first major leap in IDE capability since Smalltalk, marking an inflection point that defined a new era of development tools.
key_points: IntelliJ's key innovation was maintaining a parse tree in memory during editing, enabling intelligent assistance beyond simple text manipulation
The tool converted static typing from a compiler punishment into a helpful editing aid through type-aware completion
IntelliJ achieved something remarkable: unifying opinionated developers at Thoughtworks who previously required 'tear-gas to control the riots' over IDE choices
Eclipse is acknowledged as good but framed as a 'post-IntelliJ IDE' - a compliment that positions IntelliJ as the defining standard
Fowler had long championed Smalltalk's IDE superiority; IntelliJ was the first tool to make him stop that comparison
money_quotes: Suddenly static typing isn't just something the compiler spanks you with - now the editor can give you type correct method completion.
If anyone suggested a standard IDE for Thoughtworks projects we needed tear-gas to control the riots.
Within six months nearly everyone was using IntelliJ. Voluntarily and eagerly.
For me IntelliJ was first leap forwards in IDEs since Smalltalk.
I complement Eclipse by calling it a post-IntelliJ IDE.
Whatever happens in the future, I'll always see IntelliJ as the inflection point of IDEs, a milestone tool.
key_insight: IntelliJ represented a paradigm shift in development tools by using real-time code understanding to transform static typing from a constraint into an intelligent assistant.
